--- SOURCE 1: The role of roots on carbon sequestration ---
URL: https://jofnm.com/article-258-The-role-of-roots-on-carbon-sequestration.html

SUMMARY:
The second important aspect of carbon sequestration is that soil organic carbon (or soil organic matter) improves soil health by promoting water and nutrient retention, which are essential to plant and soil microbes’ growth. Plant roots provide soil organic carbon primarily in the form of root litter and the release of organic material, including exudates, dead cells, and mycorrhizal biomass. Roots can also contribute to organic carbon input by forming soil aggregates and protecting organic carbon from the act of microbial decomposition. The region between plant roots and the soil in which plants grow is often referred to as the rhizosphere, and the process of carbon addition directly from roots is called rhizodeposition. The rise in soil organic matter via roots results in more atmospheric carbon storage.

FULL CONTENT:
Sections

The role of roots on carbon sequestration

Over time, we can reduce emissions and capture more carbon in agricultural fields.

The author is an assistant professor at The Ohio State University.





Global warming is the rise in heat retention within the atmosphere, caused by the buildup of greenhouse gas (GHG). The consequence of global warming is climate change, which in practice results in the higher frequency and intensity of extreme weather events, such as drought, storms, flooding, and others. These extreme weather events can have a direct impact on production agriculture.

Nearly two-thirds of atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) is the result of burning fossil fuels. The remainder comes from deforestation or food production. Other important agricultural GHG are methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O). Methane is primarily emitted from ruminant animals’ digestive systems, during manure application, and when manure is in storage. Nitrous oxide is emitted mainly from fertilizer application and urine deposition in soils.

The solution to climate change involves reducing the emissions of GHG and improving carbon (C) sequestration. Reducing emissions from CH4 and N2O is more difficult than reducing CO2 because both CH4 and N2O result from complex biological processes in agricultural soils. Carbon sequestration represents uptake of carbon from the atmosphere into the soil, creating a negative flow of emissions that can be used to balance out CH4 and N2O emissions in reducing the total carbon footprint.

Long-term storage

Carbon sequestration is the process of capture, via photosynthesis, and long-term storage of atmospheric CO2. Photosynthesis and plant growth draw carbon into plant cells, releasing oxygen. Once plants die, plant residues are decomposed by soil organisms (such as bacteria, fungi, and earth worms), transforming the plant material into organic matter. Carbon is also added to the soil system by plant roots through root death, root exudates, and root respiration.

As organic matter in the topsoil layers increases over time, it slowly moves down into the soil profile and becomes more stable. Eventually, the soil organic matter is further decomposed into a more stable form of organic matter known as humus. The accumulation of organic carbon in stable organic matter at deeper soil layers represent carbon stock. The carbon stock in the soil prevents it from being emitted to the atmosphere again.

The second important aspect of carbon sequestration is that soil organic carbon (or soil organic matter) improves soil health by promoting water and nutrient retention, which are essential to plant and soil microbes’ growth. Better soil health will, then, enhance farm productivity, as well as reduce CO2 in the atmosphere.

Carbon addition via roots

Plant roots provide soil organic carbon primarily in the form of root litter and the release of organic material, including exudates, dead cells, and mycorrhizal biomass. Roots can also contribute to organic carbon input by forming soil aggregates and protecting organic carbon from the act of microbial decomposition. The region between plant roots and the soil in which plants grow is often referred to as the rhizosphere, and the process of carbon addition directly from roots is called rhizodeposition.

There are two main processes that add carbon to the soil:

1. Accumulation of organic matter and conversion of organic matter in humus.

2. Root exudates and other root-borne organic substances released into the rhizosphere during plant growth and sloughing of root hairs and fine roots.

The carbon inputs directly via roots result in organic carbon gain, particularly when the input is of stable organic matter. It has been reported that photosynthetically fixed carbon in cereals and grasses is rapidly transported toward the root, reaching the soil within one hour. During one vegetative season, cereals and grasses can allocate about 1,400 to 2,000 pounds of carbon per acre below the ground. This accounts for nearly 5% to 20% of all photosynthetically fixed carbon transferred to soils through root exudates.

Taking a closer look

Recently, the importance and role of plant roots to carbon sequestration has been deeply investigated. It has become clear that organic matter inputs from roots contribute to organic carbon stabilization much more than aboveground plant inputs. More than 200 carbon compounds released from plant roots through rhizodeposition are reported.

The compounds released by plant roots that deposit in the root sphere are many and complex, ranging from mucilage, root border cells, extracellular enzymes, simple and complex sugars, amino acids, and vitamins. Belowground inputs are up to five times more efficient on stabilizing soil organic matter than aboveground residues. The carbon storage capacity of the soil is twice compared to the atmospheric carbon.

The rise in soil organic matter via roots results in more atmospheric carbon storage. More soil organic matter also leads to greater soil fertility, better soil tilth, water-holding capacity, and reduced soil erosion. It makes plants, and the entire agricultural field, more resistant to stress and better able to withstand the climatic fluctuations that are expected to happen because of climate change.

Strategies for sequestration

The carbon content of most agricultural soils is now about one-third less than its native condition of either forest or grassland. Fortunately, modern agriculture has changed this scenario, reduced the net loss of soil carbon, and provided multiple options of strategies to restore soil carbon.

Practices that increase root growth and amount will intensify the carbon addition by roots to soils. Crop species with greater roots can deposit carbon in deeper layers — where it is protected from tillage and erosion — and contribute to carbon stocks. Introduction of perennial crops elevate carbon sequestration through root growth and cut down in soil disturbance. The use of cover crops during fallow periods provides year-round carbon sequestration and reduces soil loss via erosion. By reducing tillage, soil exposure to air is reduced, slowing down the decomposition of organic matter, which releases CO2 back to the atmosphere.

The addition of legumes into grass pastures has positive effects on both plant yield and root production. Legumes, due to their capacity of biologically fixing nitrogen (N), provide additional nitrogen supply to the grass growing in the mix. The grass will uptake nitrogen, reducing its content in the soil, favoring legumes to continue nitrogen fixation. The positive interaction between these different plant types results in greater yield and stability of production.

Higher levels of biomass production, the return of greater proportions of crop residue to the soil, and diversification (via cover crops and mix of species, such as grass-legumes in pastures) provide soil protection from loss. They also result in greater inputs of carbon sequestration.

--- SOURCE 3: Carbon Sequestration in Grasslands | MN Board of Water, Soil ... ---
URL: https://www-dev.bwsr.state.mn.us/carbon-sequestration-grasslands

SUMMARY:
Deep root systems deposit carbon into deep soil layers, which is important because the rate of carbon sequestration increases with soil depth .

FULL CONTENT:
![Minnesota Board of Water and Soil Home](/sites/default/files/logo_c.png)

## Top Bar

# Carbon Sequestration in Grasslands

Prairie systems contain much more soil organic carbon than other ecosystems due to rooting characteristics of the vegetation that grows there. These systems have adapted to frequent fire and grazing by developing deep root systems. Grasslands and shrubland carbon stock make up 34% of all carbon in the U.S. Great Plains region.1

## Deep Root Systems

Native grassland species have extensive root systems, some growing up to 15 feet deep. In fact, most of native grassland species' biomass is found below ground. Grasslands dominated by native species store more carbon in soil than those dominated by non-native species. Native prairie species are known to have up to twice the deep root biomass of introduced species.1

Deep root systems deposit carbon into deep soil layers, which is important because the rate of carbon sequestration increases with soil depth.2 Deep roots of native species are more likely to contribute to very stable carbon pools1 via  the exchange of plant sugars for nutrients with associated soil microbial life in the root zones. Biomass of soil microbes increases with plant species diversity.3 Late successional plant diversity increases carbon in the root zones as more diverse, perennial plant communities promote more diverse micro-organisms which contribute to long term, below ground carbon storage.4

![diagram showing carbon storage in plant biomass and soil organic carbon and losses of carbon through erosion and over-grazing](/sites/default/files/2019-10/Grasslands-carbon_seq_image2.PNG)

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## 

## Root Turnover

Each year as much as one-half of a native prairie plant's root system dies and regenerates. This annual root turnover contributes large amounts of organic materials to the soil system. Living roots also contribute organic material through exudation of carbohydrate-containing compounds and sloughing dead cells. Root associative fungi and bacteria exchange carbohydrates for minerals and water, over time shifts in community assembly shift towards fungal dominated biota.4 Root-derived soil organic carbon is well protected against oxidation and decay because it is buried deep in the soil profile.

## Disturbance - a hindrance and a help

Disturbance, depending on the type, can either reduce or enhance soil organic carbon accumulation. Disturbance to prairie soils from tillage results in losses of soil organic carbon from oxidation to the atmosphere in the form of carbon dioxide. Some prairie soils have lost more than 50% of their organic matter as a result of breaking the sod and subsequent tillage for crop production. Soil organic carbon is protected from oxidation and release to the atmosphere when disturbance from tillage is eliminated.4

Naturally occurring periodic fires increase the carbon storage of prairies. Fire, a natural disturbance to prairie systems, causes oxidation of organic carbon while simultaneously stimulating root growth and increasing organic matter additions to the soil. Fire results in a net gain of carbon to the soil system because enhanced root growth exceeds oxidative losses. Woody plant encroachment is controlled by periodic fire, but could actually increase carbon sequestration while altering grassland composition.1

Managed grazing can also increase carbon storage, rotational grazing practices can stimulate root growth. This practice is most effective at building soil carbon stocks when grazing management prevents overgrazing and over compaction which can reverse the sequestration rates of grassland soils by depleting vegetation and preventing healthy vegetative growth.1

1. USGCRP. Second State of the Carbon Cycle Report. (2018). Available at: <https://carbon2018.globalchange.gov/chapter/10/> (Accessed: 21st June 2019)

 2. Gill, R.A. and I.C. Burke. Plant and Soil (2002) 241:233-2423.

3. Lange, Markus, et al. “Plant diversity increases soil microbial activity and soil carbon storage” 2015. Nature Communications. 6, 6707 (2015).

4. Yang, Y., Tilman, D., Furey, G. & Lehman, C. Soil carbon sequestration accelerated by restoration of grassland biodiversity. Nat Commun 10, 718 (2019).

FULL CONTENT:
An overall review on influence of root architecture on soil carbon sequestration potential

You have full access to this open access article

2060 Accesses

5 Citations

Explore all metrics

A Correction to this article was published on 27 November 2024

This article has been updated

Abstract

Soil carbon sequestration is a vital ecosystem function that mitigates climate change by absorbing atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2). Root characteristics such as depth, diameter, length, and branching pattern affect soil carbon dynamics through root-soil interactions and organic matter breakdown. Here we review field surveys, laboratory analysis, and mathematical modeling to understand how root structures affect soil carbon storage. Further, certain root features increase soil carbon sequestration, suggesting that selective breeding and genetic engineering of plants could maximize this ecological benefit. However, more research is needed to understand the complex interactions between roots, soil biota, and soil organic matter under changing environmental conditions. In addition, the benefit of climate change mitigation methods and soil carbon models from the inclusion of root architecture was reviewed. Studies in the realm of root-soil interactions encompass a variety of academic fields, including agronomy, ecology, soil science, and plant physiology. Insights into how roots interact with their soil environment and the effects of these interactions on plant health, agricultural productivity, and environmental sustainability have been gained through this research.

1 Introduction

Root architecture describes how a plant’s root system is arranged spatially, taking into account factors like root depth, diameter, length, and branching pattern. These root characteristics may improve the soil’s ability to store carbon by having a variety of effects on soil carbon dynamics (Hooker et al. 2005). The main pathway for transporting atmospheric carbon into the soil is through the plant’s roots, which the plant uses roots for photosynthesis (Forseth 2010). While some of this carbon is absorbed into the roots, the majority is vented as root exudates into the soil (Ma et al. 2022). These exudates, which are made up of different organic compounds, can encourage soil microbial activity and help create stable soil organic matter, which is an important part of the soil carbon pool (Lehmann and Kleber 2015; Panchal et al. 2022).

Further, the physicochemical makeup of the soil can also be influenced by root architecture as deep-rooted plants can move carbon to deeper soil layers which reduces the likelihood of carbon break down and release into the atmosphere (Lucas et al. 2023). Additionally, plants with deep roots can strengthen the soil’s structure, increasing the soil’s ability to hold onto organic matter and store more carbon. The interactions between plants and soil organisms, such as mycorrhizal fungi and bacteria, which are essential to the soil’s carbon cycle, can be influenced by root design (Fitter and Garbaye 1994). The assimilation and stabilization of CO2 in the soil may be facilitated by specific root characteristics that support symbiotic partnerships with these organisms.

Figure 1 shows the carbon sequestration of CO2 with plant, soil and soil biota. Atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) is removed from the atmosphere and stored in the soil’s carbon pool through a process known as soil carbon sequestration (Washbourne et al. 2012). This procedure is a crucial ecosystem service that aids in the mitigation of climate change and plays a big part in the global carbon cycle (Pessarrodona et al. 2023). During photosynthesis, plants take in carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and transform it into organic compounds like sugars (Kuzyakov and Gavrichkova 2010). While some of these substances are utilized by the plant to thrive, others are released into the soil by the roots. By decomposing these secreted substances along with dead plant debris, soil organisms create soil organic matter, a stable type of organic matter in the soil. This organic material contributes significantly to the soil’s carbon pool and is carbon-rich (Washbourne et al. 2012).

Carbon sequestration with plant, soil and soil biota

In addition to lowering atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations and reducing global warming, soil carbon sequestration can also enhance soil health (Kim et al. 2023). By enhancing soil structure, water retention, nutrient cycling, and soil biodiversity, increased soil carbon can increase agricultural productivity and drought resistance (Lal 2006). However, depending on a number of variables, such as the type of plant, soil type, climate, and land management techniques, the pace and capacity of soil carbon sequestration can vary greatly (Hansen et al. 2001). For instance, it has been discovered that techniques like cover crops, reduced tillage management, and compost application boost soil carbon sequestration (Krauss et al. 2022; Lessmann et al. 2022). Therefore, it is essential to understand the elements that affect soil carbon sequestration, particularly root architecture for effective measures for climate change mitigation (Bruce et al. 2022).

The mitigation of climate change depends critically on soil carbon sequestration. Soils have the ability to absorb large volumes of CO2, acting as a natural sink for this greenhouse gas and lowering the atmospheric concentration (Liang et al. 2022). According to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), soil carbon sequestration is a crucial tactic for balancing greenhouse gas emissions and achieving the Paris Agreement’s goal of keeping global warming well below 2 °C (Tol 2016). The absorption of CO2 by plants during photosynthesis, its transformation into organic carbon, and its eventual storage in the soil are all parts of the process of soil carbon sequestration (Liang et al. 2022). By offsetting emissions from the burning of fossil fuels and deforestation, which are the main causes of an increase in atmospheric CO2, this activity contributes to the global carbon cycle’s balance (Landry and Matthews 2016). Soil carbon sequestration aids in reducing the overall atmospheric CO2 concentrations, which lessens the greenhouse effect and slows the rate of global warming (Washbourne et al. 2012).

Figure 2 shows the applications of the study of root soil interactions and the major factors influencing root architecture. In addition, there are numerous additional advantages of soil carbon sequestration that aid in coping with climate change (Liang et al. 2022). The health and fertility of the soil are improved with increased soil organic carbon, which raises agricultural production. Crops can resist droughts thanks to the greater water-holding ability of healthier soils with higher organic matter concentration (Bot and Benites 2005). This is crucial because it is predicted that the frequency and severity of extreme weather events, such as droughts, will rise due to shifting climatic trends (Sadhukhan et al. 2024; Srivastava et al. 2022). Finally, soil carbon sequestration is a practical and affordable method of reducing greenhouse gas emissions (Rodrigues et al. 2021). Numerous managements including cover crops, conservation tillage, and agroforestry can be incorporated into current agricultural systems to increase soil carbon sequestration (Rice and Reed 2007). As a result, encouraging soil carbon sequestration is consistent with the objectives of sustainable development, offering a strategy to reduce climate change while also supporting food security and rural lives (Davidson et al. 2003; Branca et al. 2013). However, the importance of soil carbon sequestration in mitigating global warming cannot be overstated (Searchinger and Ranganathan 2020). It is a natural solution that promotes soil health, agricultural resilience, and sustainable development in addition to lowering atmospheric CO2 concentrations. In our joint fight against climate change, we may advance significantly by giving priority to actions that increase soil carbon sequestration (Krauss et al. 2022). Therefore, studies on the interactions between roots and soil could be a pathway for establishing the connections between plants, soil microbes, nutrient cycling, to soil carbon sequestration (Singh et al. 2004).

Applications of the study of root soil interactions and the major factors influencing root architecture

Although research on this subject is still in process, we can create more efficient solutions for mitigating climate change by comprehending and taking advantage of the impact of root design on soil carbon storage (Herms et al. 2022). This could involve breeding or genetically modifying plants to have features in their roots that improve soil carbon storage (Lawlor 2013). To completely comprehend the intricate relationships between root architecture, soil organisms, and carbon cycling under various climatic situations, additional research is nonetheless required (Poirier et al. 2017, 2018). Significant potential exists for improving soil carbon sequestration in the plant roots. Recognizing and utilizing this potential may be essential to promote sustainable land management through soil carbon sequestration and slow down the effects of climate change (Washbourne et al. 2012).

2 Root architecture: an overview

Root architecture describes the physical configuration and layout of a plant’s root system. It is a crucial component of plant morphology and is crucial to a plant’s capacity to absorb water and nutrients from the soil (Frescht et al. 2021). In order to increase soil carbon storage, a number of root characteristics have been identified (Table 1). These characteristics have the potential to affect the physical and biological processes that control carbon retention, as well as the quantity and quality of carbon inputs to the soil (Ahmed et al. 2019). Different root characteristics can affect how much soil carbon is stored. However, there are several variables, including soil type, climate, and land management techniques, that might affect the connections between these features and carbon storage.

A plant’s root system can be roughly broken down into essential components that contribute to the root architecture such as shape, size, and structure of the roots, as well as their depth, diameter, branching pattern, and dispersion within the soil profile (Ma et al. 2022). The first root to emerge from the seed during germination is the primary root, often referred to as the taproot in some plants. It usually develops downward and acts as the plant’s primary ground anchor. Secondary roots break off from the main root and expand laterally to different degrees (Samuel and Dines 2023). The tertiary roots of these secondary roots may branch out further to form a complex soil network (Frescht et al. 2021). The root hairs, which are the finest and farthest components of this network, greatly enhance the root surface area available for water and nutrient uptake (Choi and Cho 2019). Even within the same plant species, root architecture can differ significantly between different individuals due to their genetic and environmental variables, and understanding these factors critically can pave a way for root architecture influence on how plants grow, develop, and adapt to their surroundings (Frescht et al. 2021).

The roots can be divided based on the diameter cutoff i.e., coarse roots which have diameter of more than 2 mm, and fine roots which have the diameter of less than 2 mm. Coarse roots help with providing plant anchorage and nutrient storage, while fine roots help with the assimilation of nutrients and water along with their transport in different parts of the plant. Zhang and Wang (2015) reported that with a decrease in the depth of soil, the root decomposition rates decrease. So, fine roots decompose faster than coarse roots because of the lower C:N ratio and lignin concentration (Silver and Miya 2001). However, little is known about how root morphology affects decomposition. Based on the current studies by some of the researchers (Freschet and Roumet 2017; McCormack et al. 2015; Poirier et al. 2017, 2018) there may be a positive correlation between a particular root length and decomposition for plants with roots upto 40 cm. Nevertheless, the variability of the root pool (fine or coarse) under consideration may lead to methodological bias and inconsistency in the results. As a point of fact, some of the current studies emphasized the existence of many root orders with varying structures, functions, and effects on soil processes among roots smaller than 2 mm (Fan and Guo 2010; McCormack et al. 2015). For instance, the more distal first- and second-order roots decompose more slowly than the third- and fourth-order roots (Sun et al. 2013). This is unexpected because, in comparison to higher-order roots, lower-order roots have smaller diameters, and higher concentrations of N. Since, first-order roots have a shorter life span, so one essential factor for the soil’s preservation of carbon is a slower rate of decomposition (Fan and Guo 2010; McCormack et al. 2015). Moreover, greater root length density can also boost soil organic carbon stabilization through occlusion inside microaggregates, even though it primarily affects soil macroaggregation (Erktan et al. 2016; Ontl et al. 2015; Poirier et al. 2017). Furthermore, with different soil organic C concentrations, the effect of fine roots on macroaggregation also differed. Organic C-poor soils with more fine roots had higher macroaggregation, while soil organic C-rich soil with lesser fine roots had lower macroaggregation (Erktan et al. 2016). Therefore, the distribution of roots at different depths is a vital parameter to influence C stabilization and storage in the soil. Other characteristics of roots, including their, physiology, chemistry, architecture, and symbiosis, may have an impact on the stabilization of soil organic matter once they have penetrated deeper layers of soil. This interaction is primarily due to interactions with minerals.

A significant knowledge gap is also created by the possibility of increased root C inputs in different layers of soil (subsoil or topsoil) causing destabilization of soil organic matter. Predictions or actual findings regarding the equilibrium between destabilization caused by root growth and stabilization of soil organic matter are largely unknown. Improving these forecasts is essential to clarify how roots affect the global soil C budget and how changes in vegetation communities may affect soil organic matter in ecosystems  (Iversen 2010; Laliberté, 2017; Stockmann et al. 2013).

Moreover, different plant species and cultivars have innate genetic programs that influence the formation of their root systems (Lynch et al. 2021). Numerous genes connected to branching and root growth have been discovered by scientists (Moreno-Risueno et al. 2010; Nibau et al. 2008; Qin et al. 2022). For example, certain genes control the commencement and development of lateral roots, while others manage the growth of root hair. Root architecture can change significantly as a result of mutations in these genes (Julkowska et al. 2017).

Further, root architecture is also strongly influenced by environmental influences as well and these factors consist of soil characteristics, water and nutrient availability, temperature, and interactions with soil organisms (Julkowska et al. 2017). In order to maximize nutrient intake, plants growing in nutrient-poor soils frequently produce broad root systems, whereas plants growing in dry settings may have deep roots to obtain water at deeper soil layers (Wendel et al. 2022). Regarding resource acquisition, stress resistance, and other elements of plant development and survival, diverse root designs offer various benefits and drawbacks (Samuel and Dines 2023). For instance, strong anchoring can be provided by a deep, thick taproot, but it may be less efficient at absorbing nutrients that are more prevalent in the upper soil layers (Julkowska et al. 2017). A shallow, heavily branching root system, on the other hand, may be stronger at absorbing nutrients but more vulnerable to drought (Abd Allah et al. 2010). A complicated characteristic that is essential in the interactions between plants and their environment is root architecture (Frescht et al. 2021). Understanding root design can help us better understand plant ecology, agronomy, and methods for reducing greenhouse gas emissions such as soil carbon sequestration (Ma et al. 2022).

Root growth and development may be impacted by the physical and chemical characteristics of the soil as the dense or compacted clay soils can limit root growth, resulting in shorter and less branching root systems (Rich and Watt 2013). In contrast, roots can grow more freely in loose, well-drained soils, leading to deeper, extensively branching root systems. In general, roots expand in directions where water and important nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium are more readily available (Ma et al. 2022). In order to enhance resource uptake in dry weather or nutrient-poor soils, plants frequently develop deeper or more branching root systems (Samuel and Dines 2023). The pace of root growth and structural integrity may be impacted by soil temperature (30 °C) (Ingram et al. 2015). Furthermore, optimal root growth typically takes place within this range while temperatures outside of a certain range can also prevent root formation (Calleja-Cabrera et al. 2020). Additionally, interactions with soil microbes like fungi and bacteria can affect the root architecture (Sukumar et al. 2013). Some of these microbes interact symbiotically with plant roots, creating mycorrhizae that can improve nutrient intake and influence root growth patterns (Jacoby et al. 2017).

3 Roles of plant hormones in root architecture

Auxins, cytokinins, and gibberellins are examples of plant hormones that are important for root formation (Vissenberg et al. 2020). These hormones control a number of root architecture-related activities, such as cell division, elongation, and differentiation, as well as how plants react to their surroundings (Vissenberg et al. 2020). The complex property of root architecture is influenced by a complex interplay of hereditary and environmental variables (Brook 2009). Understanding these factors can help in formulating plans to manage soil health and carbon sequestration, as well as to increase plant productivity and resilience (Harmen et al. 2021).

There is a striking amount of species-to-species variety in the design of plant roots. This diversity illustrates how plants have evolved to adapt to a variety of environmental factors and ecological niches (Nagel et al. 2009). Species with a taproot system, defined by a large primary root from which smaller lateral roots sprout, are at one extreme of the spectrum (Harmen et al. 2021). Many dicotyledonous plants, including oaks and carrots, have this style of root architecture (Pages 2011). In arid or nutrient-poor settings, a deep taproot can offer stable anchorage and access to water and minerals buried deep in the soil (Pages 2011). Contrarily, many monocotyledonous plants have a fibrous root structure, including grasses and cereals (Maqbool et al. 2022). There is no dominant major root in this style of construction. Instead, the plant has a large number of fine roots < 2 mm diameter (Poirier et al. 2017, 2018) that are widely dispersed close to the soil’s surface (Mickovski and Van Beek 2009). In settings where these resources are abundant near the surface or quickly leach with rainfall, a fibrous root system is good at absorbing water and nutrients from the upper soil layers. Fibrous root systems are linked to several root morphological characteristics, including precise root length and root diameter. The main supply of carbon that stabilizes soil is the number of decaying roots (Poirier et al. 2017, 2018). Nonetheless, root morphological features (e.g., the diameter of root, specific root length) are also important factors that control the decomposition rates of the root (Goebel et al. 2011). Specifically, these morphological traits will be crucial in determining the significance of these various mechanisms of root decay, which will have an impact on C stabilization. However, the results related to the real time decomposition of roots cannot be achieved in the studies conducted so far because of design limits during studies that can maintain direct contact between soil and the roots (Jackson et al. 2017; Li et al. 2015).

Given their effects on soil organic matter, root breakdown mechanisms must be understood. The surfaces of roots are exposed to increased microbial activity and minerals, which are crucial for the preservation of soil organic matter. Therefore, root morphology may be especially significant to comprehend the whole picture of soil C dynamics (Lehmann and Kleber 2015; Zhang et al. 2008; Wang et al. 2021). Here, we discovered that a complicated relationship between root morphology and organic C, does, alter root decomposition rates. Additionally, root morphology affects the organic C loss during the decomposition of root. The generality of these findings will require more research, but these variations in the decomposition routes imply that root shape plays a significant role in regulating root C stabilization (Wallenstein et al. 2013; Osono et al. 2014). Furthermore, research on root breakdown with direct exposure to soil becomes crucial given the recent interactions with minerals during soil C stabilization (Fan and Guo 2010; Lehmann and Kleber 2015). Moreover, organic C concentration in the roots is sometimes blamed for slower degradation of roots (Beidler and Pritchard 2017; Wang et al. 2021). In light of recent discoveries on the environmental constraints on root traits, the intriguing possibility of connecting species traits to root decomposition mechanisms and soil C dynamics merits more research (Valverde-Barrantes et al. 2015; Yin et al. 2021).

Some plant species have evolved specific root structures to improve nutrient uptake, especially those acclimated to highly nutrient-poor situations. Examples of dense collections of short lateral roots that enhance root surface area and leak organic substances to mobilize nutrients in the soil include cluster roots in some Proteaceae species and dauciform roots in Cyperaceae (Lynch et al. 2012). Plants that collaborate symbiotically with soil fungi to create mycorrhizae, which drastically modify root architecture, exhibit another noteworthy diversity. For instance, the fungal partner creates a sheath around the root tips in ectomycorrhizal partnerships, which are generally found in tree species like pine and oaks, whereas in arbuscular mycorrhizal associations, which are widespread in many grasses and crops, the fungi enter the root cells (Sharma 2017; Chen et al. 2014). These illustrations show the wide range of root architecture among various plant species. Understanding this diversity, as well as the environmental factors and evolutionary pressures that influence it, can help us better understand plant ecology, agricultural methods, and climate change mitigation techniques (Sharma 2017).

4 The role of root architecture in soil carbon sequestration

Plants use photosynthesis to take atmospheric CO2 and turn it into organic molecules and these substances are utilized to construct plant tissues, such as roots (Taub 2010). The pool of soil organic carbon is increased by the growth, death, and decomposition of roots. Because carbon is less likely to be lost through decomposition in the deeper soil layers, plants with larger and deeper root systems may be able to sequester more of it there (Kirschbaum et al. 2021). The effect of roots on the physical characteristics of soil has also been studied in research (Gould et al. 2016; Mosaddeghi et al. 2009). As they move through the soil, roots can change the soil’s structure by forming pores and channels that enhance air and water infiltration. Interest has also been shown in the function of roots in the creation of soil aggregates, which can prevent organic matter from decomposing and support carbon storage (Basile-Doelsch et al. 2020).

Root exudates and their function in the soil carbon cycle are likewise gaining more and more attention (Bardgett et al. 2013). Organic substances called root exudates, which are released by roots into the soil, are essential for soil carbon sequestration (Kirschbaum et al. 2021). For soil microorganisms, these exudates act as a food supply, promoting their activity (Ma et al. 2022). These microbes convert part of the carbon into more stable forms that are resistant to decomposition as they eat root exudates and other organic components, aiding in the long-term storage of carbon in the soil (Kane and Solutions 2015). Root architecture can affect the amount and makeup of root exudates, with some root characteristics favoring higher exudation rates (Iannucci et al. 2021). The physical makeup of the soil can be impacted by root architecture, which in turn affects the soil’s ability to retain carbon. For instance, the development of roots through the soil can produce holes and channels that enhance the soil’s structure and ability to retain water (Bartens et al. 2008). This may improve the soil’s ability to hold onto organic material and subsequently carbon. Additionally, the breakdown of roots may result in the creation of aggregates, which are clumps of bound-together soil particles that can prevent organic matter from decomposing and encourage carbon storage (Yudina and Kuzyakov 2023). Moreover, studies have been heavily focused on root exudates because of their function in nutrient cycling and their potential to improve soil fertility (Moreau et al. 2019; Abalos et al. 2019; Yudina and Kuzyakov 2023). These exudates have the ability to solubilize minerals and increase microbial activity. Therefore, the interactions between roots and soil microorganisms are another crucial field that needs to be investigated. The symbiotic interactions between roots and particular kinds of fungi (mycorrhizae) or bacteria (rhizobia) have been the subject of several investigations (Abdel-Lateif et al. 2012). These symbioses can affect soil structure and carbon cycling, as well as plant nutrient intake and stress tolerance (Gould et al. 2016). Researchers have been able to develop further into these connections via molecular biology and genetics, revealing insights into the intricate communication between plants and their microbial companions (Rane et al. 2022; Gao et al. 2022). To completely comprehend these relationships and their consequences for carbon sequestration systems, more research is required. However, there is still much to learn about the intricate relationships between root architecture, soil microbes, and carbon cycling since this field of study is still relatively new in terms of understanding global climate change (Galindo-Castañeda et al. 2022; Herms et al. 2022).

5 Future directions

Several gaps still need to be filled to fully grasp the function that root design plays in soil carbon sequestration, despite major gains in our understanding. One critical knowledge gap is the need for a deeper understanding of the particular root characteristics that have the greatest impact on soil carbon sequestration (Mcleod et al. 2011). The complicated interactions between these qualities and environmental factors make it difficult to determine which traits are most relevant in a given situation, even though we have identified some traits like root depth, root biomass, and root exudation as potentially important. These connections need to be clarified, and potential trade-offs between various fundamental features need to be identified. The requirement for greater quantitative information on root properties across a wide range of plant species is a related gap (Colmer 2003). Current root characteristic databases are sparse and frequently favor certain plant types, such as temperate tree species or crops. These databases might be considerably improved by including a wider variety of plant species, such as tropical trees, grasses, and perennial crops, to better understand the heterogeneity in root characteristics and their effects on soil carbon sequestration (Kattge et al. 2020).

Another area that needs more research is the function of root exudates in the cycling of soil carbon. Although it is generally recognized that root exudates can promote microbial activity and aid in the development of stable soil organic matter, the precise mechanisms at play and the impact of various exudate forms are not fully understood (Lei et al. 2023). Advanced methods like isotopic tracing of carbon flow and metabolomic investigation of root exudates could offer important insights into this field. It is also unknown how roots interact with other soil microbes, including symbiotic partners like mycorrhizal fungi and other members of the soil microbiome. Future studies could look into how root characteristics affect these interactions and how soil carbon dynamics are impacted by them (Van Hees et al. 2005).

More study is required to link smaller-scale interactions in the rhizosphere, the area of soil where roots have an influence, to more significant processes like ecosystem carbon cycling and climate change (Sokol et al. 2022). This could entail creating models

# Root architecture and carbon sequestration potential of fast-growing agroforestry tree species in semi-arid Central India

![Kamini Gautam](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/3050288/overview/74)
![Naresh Kumar*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/3042318/overview/74)
![Asha Ram*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1858405/overview/74)
![Inder Dev](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)
![Burhan U. Choudhury](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2103015/overview/74)
![Nongmaithem Raju Singh](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2728543/overview/74)
![Arun Kumar Handa](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)
![Ashok Yadav](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/856307/overview/74)
![Hirdayesh Anuragi](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1698182/overview/74)
![A. R. Uthappa](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1649133/overview/74)
![Dhiraj Kumar](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2045798/overview/74)
![Ayyanadar Arunachalam](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/152312/overview/74)
![Dinesh Jinger](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1683220/overview/74)

Understanding the link between tree root architecture and organic carbon dynamics is critical for enhancing carbon sequestration in semi-arid regions. This study, conducted from 2017 to 2019 in central India, evaluated the root structure and carbon sequestration potential of three tree species: *Neolamarckia cadamba* (Kadam), *Leucaena leucocephala* (Subabul), and *Melia dubia* (Malabar neem). The species exhibited distinct root architectures: Subabul had a symmetric, sparse root system; Kadam had moderately dense roots; and Malabar neem developed a compact and massive root system. The highest root density was recorded in the 0–30 cm topsoil layer near the collar region. Primary roots initially grew vertically (0.15–0.30 m), then extended horizontally, with Malabar neem showing the widest lateral spread (up to 4.4 m). Secondary roots displayed greater angular spread than tertiary and quaternary roots. Lateral root pruning, recommended after the first two years, could enhance resource use efficiency and improve understory crop performance in agroforestry systems. Malabar neem demonstrated significantly higher carbon sequestration potential, storing 25.64 Mg C ha-¹ at three years—2.96 to 3.86 times greater than Subabul (8.62 Mg C ha-¹) and Kadam (6.62 Mg C ha-¹). Annual sequestration rates ranged from 2.20 to 2.87 Mg C ha-¹ yr-¹. Aboveground biomass contributed 80.4–84.3% of total carbon stocks, with belowground biomass contributing 15.7–19.6%. At a planting density of 500 trees ha-¹, Malabar neem achieved the highest CO2-equivalent sequestration (94.09 Mg CO2e ha-¹). These findings highlight Malabar neem-based agroforestry as a viable strategy for restoring degraded lands while improving carbon storage and climate resilience in semi-arid ecosystems.

## Introduction

Agroforestry is a holistic and multifunctional land use system that has been widely acknowledged for enhancing both livelihood security and environmental sustainability, particularly in developing countries such as India ([Jemal et al., 2018](#B35); [Kmoch et al., 2018](#B45); [Ansari et al., 2022](#B4); [Jinger et al., 2023](#B39)). Its perennial component play a vital role in combating climate change by absorbing atmospheric carbon dioxide, regulating temperatures, conserving soil and water, and supporting biodiversity. They act as natural carbon sinks, reduce the effects of extreme weather, and provide renewable resources, making them essential for both climate mitigation and adaptation. By integrating trees with crops and/or livestock, agroforestry systems not only capture atmospheric CO2 but also improve soil health, reduce erosion, and increase farm productivity—making them powerful tools for climate change mitigation and sustainable land management. Few recent studies have highlighted the carbon sequestration potential of the agroforestry systems in both soil and biomass ([Bettles et al., 2021](#B7); [Siarudin et al., 2021](#B61); [Singh et al., 2024](#B63)). As India has taken a pioneering role in promoting agroforestry by becoming the first country to introduce a National Agroforestry Policy in 2014. This policy has since been integrated into several national initiatives, including the National Bamboo Mission, National Biofuel Policy, Green India Mission, National Horticulture Mission, and the National Mission on Sustainable Agriculture. As of 2023, approximately 28.42 million hectares of land in India are under agroforestry ([Ram et al., 2023](#B55)). Moreover, land suitability assessments reveal that 32.8%, 40.0%, and 11.7% of the country’s total land area are classified as highly, moderately, and marginally suitable, respectively, for the expansion of agroforestry ([Ahmad et al., 2019](#B2)). Evidently, the strategic promotion of agroforestry could play a crucial role in achieving India’s target of 33% forest cover ([Dhyani et al., 2016](#B22); [Sahoo and Wani, 2019](#B57); [Dhyani et al., 2021](#B20)).

To ensure the success of agroforestry systems, it is essential to evaluate the structural and developmental characteristics of tree root systems, as they play a vital role in enhancing and optimizing overall system productivity ([Arunkumar and Chauhan, 2018](#B5)). Root architecture studies are crucial for selecting tree species that can effectively survive, establish, and grow under challenging agroclimatic and edaphic conditions, as they influences a plant’s ability to access water and nutrients, thereby enhancing resilience and sustainability in forestry and agroforestry systems ([Ma et al., 2024](#B48)). In agroforestry, understanding root architecture helps reduce competition with crops, improve soil health, and enhance carbon sequestration, contributing to sustainable and climate-resilient land use. Minimizing competition between trees and associated agricultural components such as seasonal crops, semi-perennial fruit trees, or fodder is critical for developing compatible agroforestry models. In this regard, root system manipulation becomes a key intervention to reduce competition for belowground resources such as water and nutrients. Despite of having a crucial role of root architecture in the growth and productivity of a system, no enough studies have been conducted on the topic so far therefore a systematic studies on structure of tree roots urgently required to unveil important insights into belowground carbon sequestration and other ecosystem services. Understanding root architecture dynamics can aid in the selection of suitable tree species for designing productive and resilient agroforestry systems. Such knowledge allows for the targeted exploitation and manipulation of root traits to improve both tree and crop yields, thereby contributing to optimized land use ([Herder et al., 2010](#B31); [Kaushal et al., 2019](#B44)).

The commercial success of fast-growing tree species such as *Populus* and *Eucalyptus* in the states of Haryana and Punjab has sparked growing interest among farmers, primarily due to their short rotation periods and early economic returns ([Chavan and Dhillon, 2019](#B14)). These species provide a sustainable alternative to low-carbon land use systems, offering high yields of renewable woody biomass along with multiple ecosystem benefits ([Bredemeier et al., 2015](#B9); [Jinger et al., 2024](#B37)). Tree species such as *Neolamarckia cadamba* (kadam), *Leucaena leucocephala* (subabul), and *Melia dubia* (malabar neem) have demonstrated strong potential for commercial agroforestry in semi-arid regions ([Chaturvedi et al., 2016](#B11); [Handa et al., 2019](#B30); [Jinger et al., 2024](#B37)). These species not only meet the increasing demand for timber, pulpwood, fuelwood, and fodder, but also contribute to environmental restoration by conserving soil moisture, reducing erosion, restoring degraded lands, and sequestering significant amounts of atmospheric carbon dioxide—thereby improving overall farm income ([Hombegowda et al., 2020](#B32); [Ahlawat et al., 2024](#B1); [Jinger et al., 2025](#B38)). In addition, these multipurpose tree species can be effectively integrated with a wide range of food crops, vegetables, medicinal plants, fruit trees, and grasses, enhancing system diversity and resilience ([Karmini and Karyati, 2017](#B41); [Karthikeyan et al., 2018](#B42); [Thakur et al., 2019](#B65)). Planting such fast-growing tree species might not necessarily support carbon tunnel vision, as long as broader ecological and social impacts are also considered ([Santiago and Doughty, 2018](#B58)). Competition in agroforestry can lead to both positive and negative ecological consequences. While competition for resources like light, water, and nutrients may reduce the productivity of certain components, it can also drive niche differentiation and improve overall system efficiency. Properly managed competition can enhance biodiversity, promote soil health, and support ecosystem resilience. However, if unmanaged, it may lead to resource depletion and reduced yields of understory crops.

Interestingly, the semi-arid regions, which account for approximately 35–40% of India’s total geographical area, are typically characterized by low and erratic rainfall, high temperatures, and sparse vegetation cover ([Kumar et al., 2021](#B46)) where, soils are often shallow, low in organic carbon, and nutrient-deficient, making them highly susceptible to erosion and degradation ([Jinger et al., 2022](#B40)). Subsequently, degraded and wastelands are prevalent in these areas, where farmers largely rely on rainfed agriculture ([Ghosh et al., 2021](#B27)). Improving soil health and enhancing carbon sequestration in these vulnerable ecosystems is, therefore, an urgent priority. Against this backdrop, we hypothesize that the three fast-growing, multipurpose tree species—kadam, subabul, and malabar neem could serve as viable alternatives for restoring degraded lands by improving soil health and carbon sequestration potential. To test this hypothesis, the present study was conducted with the following objectives: (a) to assess the root system architecture and carbon sequestration potential of these species, and (b) to identify the most promising species for establishing climate-resilient agroforestry systems in the semi-arid tropics of India.

## Materials and methods

### Study site

The study was conducted in an area of 1.4 ha at research farm of ICAR-Central Agroforestry Research Institute (CAFRI), Jhansi, Uttar Pradesh, India during three consecutive years (2016–17 to 2018-19). The study site is geographically located between 25°30’25°32’ N latitude and 78°32’78°34’ E longitudes, and it is situated 272 m above mean sea level ([Figure 1](#f1)). The average annual rainfall of the region is around 867 mm with large temporal and spatial variability while the mean maximum temperature ranges from 23.5°C in January to 47.4°C in June, and mean minimum temperature ranges from 4.1°C in December to 27.2°C in June ([Dev et al., 2020](#B19)). The soil of the study site was a mixture of red and black soils with poor fertility. The initial (before study) soil pH, organic carbon (%), available N, P and K (kg ha-1) was 7.76, 0.48, 185.5, 8.46 and 172.3, respectively.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1597122/fagro-07-1597122-HTML-r1/image_m/fagro-07-1597122-g001.jpg)

**Figure 1**. Map of India with Bundelkhand region of Uttar Pradesh (Green) and study locations (Light Yellow).

### Experimental design and tree/crop management

With three replications, the experiment was conducted using a randomized block design (RBD) in 2016. The seedlings of kadam, malabar neem and subabul were planted at a spacing of 5.0 m (row to row) and 4.0 m (plant to plant) during August, 2016. Kadam and subabul plants were raised from seeds of local plus trees, whereas of malabar neem plants were raised from stem cuttings from local plus trees in the nursery and transplanted to the field after six months. The *Vigna mungo* (kharif) and *Triticum aestivum* (rabi) cropping system were intercropped with recommended practices during the study that ran from 2017 to 2019. For proper establishment of tree seedlings, approximately 50 liters of water were given every 15 days during the first four months. Three lifesaving irrigations were given annually during the summer season after establishment. In the wheat crop, five irrigations were given during the rabi season. To reduce crown competition, three species of trees were pruned by 50% every year (before sowing kharif and rabi crops).

### Parameters studied

#### Root architecture parameters

In the winter season, when trees undergo dormancy, data on root architecture of three tree species was collected for three consecutive years (2017**-**2019). Considering a single plant as one replication, every year, three healthy, uniform trees of each species were uprooted and studied for root architecture, totaling nine trees per species. Selected trees of each species were manually excavated ([Figure 2](#f2)) and the root morphological structures of trees were exposed using hydraulic sluicing. Hydraulic sluicing was carried out downwards from the tree stump to ensure to expose sufficient depth of root system ([Czernin and Phillips, 2005](#B16); [Phillips et al., 2014](#B54); [Marden et al., 2018](#B50)). Root system parameters were measured by following procedures given by [Czernin and Phillips (2005)](#B16) and [Marden et al. (2018)](#B50). Before uprooting, aboveground growth parameters, such as tree height, collar diameter, diameter at breast height (DBH), and crown width, were measured using a Ravi Altimeter and a digital Vernier calliper. The uprooted root system was re-aligned into original positions and all the qualitative and quantitative parameters were recorded. The qualitative parameters measured were root symmetry, root distribution pattern, and general root morphology as per the ([Czernin and Phillips, 2005](#B16); [Phillips et al., 2014](#B54); [Marden et al., 2018](#B50)). Quantitative parameters included root system spread and depth, length, number, diameter, angle, and biomass of roots from different orders, as well as root:shoot ratio, root spread, and crown spread as per the ([Czernin and Phillips, 2005](#B16); [Phillips et al., 2014](#B54)). The main root in Malabar neem was not distinct, so the most prominent central root was chosen as the main/tap root for recording various parameters in root architecture study. Secondary roots were designated for roots that originated from the main root/primary root (tap root), regardless of their size. Those that came from secondary and tertiary roots were labeled as tertiary and quaternary roots, respectively. Non-woody and white roots that come from woody secondary, tertiary, and quaternary roots with a diameter of less than 1 mm were deemed fine roots ([Czernin and Phillips, 2005](#B16); [Phillips et al., 2014](#B54)). A digital Vernier caliper, measuring tape, and scale were used to measure quantitative parameters such as horizontal spread of roots, root system depth, mean root length, and diameter. Further, the angle at which a secondary root emerges from a primary root was recorded to determine the branching angle or root fork angle ([Czernin and Phillips, 2005](#B16)). The root system was compared by calculating root and shoot biomass, crown, and root spread among the three tree species. For above- and below-ground dry biomass estimates, the samples were oven dried at 70 ± 2°C till constant weight ([Peng and Dang, 2003](#B53)). Values of individual parameters were averaged to obtain the mean values.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1597122/fagro-07-1597122-HTML-r1/image_m/fagro-07-1597122-g002.jpg)

**Figure 2**. Root architecture of Kadam **(a)**, Subabul **(b)**, and Malabar neem **(c)** at 3 years’ age.

#### Carbon stock parameters

The carbon stock (aboveground and belowground) was estimated by using the trees that were uprooted for root system architecture studies. Aboveground portions were divided into stem, leaves, and branches. The belowground portions were divided into tap/primary root, secondary, tertiary, quaternary roots, and fine roots. The fresh weight of all the tree components was taken separately in the field to avoid any variation in weight. Three samples with known fresh weights of all the tree components (species wise) were kept in an oven for drying at 70 ± 2°C till constant weight and then their oven dry weight was recorded. Carbon stock in the various parts of trees (dry biomass × 0.5) as well as their potential to mitigate carbon-dioxide (carbon stock × 3.67) was calculated ([IPCC, 2006](#B33)). All the tree parts were added to calculate the total carbon storage in biomass, which was then used to calculate carbon storage and carbon dioxide mitigation for each species on a per hectare basis.

### Statistical analysis

Statistical analysis was carried out using ANOVA and t-test to determine the effects of year and species on above- and below-ground parameters as per [Gomez and Gomez (1984)](#B28). Data were analyzed using one factor analysis using IBM SPSS (v 25.0). Quantitative data pertaining to quaternary root properties were analyzed using a t-test ([Gomez and Gomez, 1984](#B28)). The mean values were compared at a 5% level of significance (*p <*0.05).

## Results and discussion

### Tree growth parameters

Most tree growth parameters exhibited a significant difference (*p <*0.05) in all the species. At three years of age, the tree species had a height range of 5.04 to 9.92 m, while the crown spread ranged from 3.88 to 4.51 m. Malabar neem showed the best growth performance, followed by subabul and kadam ([Figure 3](#f3)). The diameter at breast height (DBH) followed a similar trend. Kadam and Malabar neem had larger collar diameters than subabul. The studied species were growing at a faster rate than common tree species in the region, such as *Azadirachta indica*, *Tectona grandis*, *Albizia procera*, *Dalbergia sissoo*, *Hardwickia binata*, and others ([Dagar and Tomar, 2002](#B17); [Loushambam et al., 2017](#B47)). This indicates their adaptability to the semi-arid environment of central India.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1597122/fagro-07-1597122-HTML-r1/image_m/fagro-07-1597122-g003.jpg)

**Figure 3**. Increased pattern (yearly) in crown spread (m), height (m), collar diameter (cm) and DBH in three tree species.

### Qualitative parameters of root system

All three tree species had well-developed root systems with maximum root density found in the topsoil (0.30 m) and close to the collar region ([Table 1](#T1)). The root system was almost symmetric in terms of an even distribution of biomass around all axes and an almost equal root spread in the NS and EW directions in these tree species. Due to hard seed coat–induced dormancy and low germination rates, *M. dubia* is commercially propagated through stem cuttings, which offer higher success and uniformity ([Jayanandan, 2016](#B34)). The roots of subabul were found to be less dense or diffuse, while those of kadam were found to be medium dense, and the roots of malabar neem were both compact and massive. This difference in rooting pattern might be also attributed to the difference in origin of root development and hormonal regulation during early growth stages ([Albrecht et al., 2017](#B3)). Subabul and kadam showed a well-developed, easily recognizable primary (tap) root system. The main root of malabar neem was not distinct, since it was raised from cuttings. Instead, the root with the most prominent central fibrous structure that became a main one was labelled as the primary (tap) root. All three species showed sturdy primary (tap) roots, with the maximum diameter at the collar region and tapering towards the end. However, the primary root grew partially vertically in the soil. Their development was limited to vertical growth until 0.3 m, then horizontal growth until 0.50-2.0 m, and then downwards because of positive geotropism. The secondary, tertiary, and quaternary root systems were present in kadam and malabar neem, while there was no quaternary root system present in subabul. Secondary roots in all these tree species grew laterally towards the surface and then displayed positive geotropism, which was also visible from wider angles of secondary roots ([Table 1](#T1)). These secondary roots produced the maximum number of fine roots. The root color of kadam and subabul was paler yellow to yellowish brown, while malabar neem was reddish brown with black streaks on its surface. The root surface of subabul had a distinct feature: small round dots that looked like superficial growth and were white. The tree species that were evaluated all showed rapid growth and a moderately deep vertical taproot growth in the soil. These tree species confirmed that they had root systems that were almost symmetrical to ensure anchorage, structural support, and stability ([Coutts et al., 1999](#B15)). The root system’s symmetric growth indicates that they are competing for below-ground resources (water, nutrients, etc.), which makes it suitable for inclusion in agroforestry systems ([Rewald and Leuschner, 2009](#B56)). Nonetheless, previous researchers have already documented a shallow root system in malabar neem ([Mulatya et al., 2002](#B51); [Arunkumar and Chauhan, 2018](#B5)). Contrary to our observations under semi-arid climate, it has been reported that the root system of subabul has a slightly deeper tap root system with maximum root density at the topsoil under humid sub-tropical climate of North-Western Himalaya ([Dhyani et al., 1990](#B21)).

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1597122/fagro-07-1597122-HTML-r1/image_m/fagro-07-1597122-t001.jpg)

**Table 1**. Quantitative root traits of fast-growing tree species.

The spread of secondary roots near the collar and topsoil, followed by vertical growth through positive geotropism, enhances tree stability by providing additional anchorage and structural support. Similar root distribution patterns have been observed in species like *Acacia auriculiformis*, *Azadirachta indica*, and *Bombax ceiba* ([Das and Chaturvedi, 2008](#B18)). The concentration of roots in the topsoil indicates potential competition with seasonal crops, which also have shallow, fibrous roots. Therefore, selecting compatible intercrops in agroforestry systems requires careful planning, along with practices like lateral root wrenching and pruning to improve resource use and reduce competition ([Chaturvedi and Das, 2002](#B10); [Das and Chaturvedi, 2008](#B18)). Additionally, the centralization of root systems near the stem enhances wind resistance and structural stability ([Stokes, 2013](#B64)).

The less dense or diffused roots systems in subabul and medium dense root systems in kadam lend themselves to integration with agricultural crops in an agroforestry system. The massive and compact root system of malabar neem indicates its suitability for wasteland restoration. This tree species’ extensive root network in the surface soil may improve soil fertility and aggregation while also preventing erosion and topsoil loss ([Das and Chaturvedi, 2008](#B18); [Verma et al., 2014](#B67); [Borden et al., 2016](#B8)). For establishing malabar neem-based agroforestry and silvipastural systems, spacing between tree rows needs to be kept wider to avoid shading effects and competition for underground resources on associated crops ([Das and Chaturvedi, 2008](#B18); [Verma et al., 2014](#B67)).

### Quantitative root system parameters

In the 3rd year of the study (2019) when trees attained 3-year, kadam (248 cm) had a longer primary (tap) root, followed by malabar neem (211 cm) and subabul (208*cm*) ([Table 1](#T1)). The primary (tap) roots of all three tree species grew vertically only within the first 0.30m of soil depth, then spread horizontally across a distance of 0.50 to 20m from their source, and finally exhibited positive geotropism. At the age of 3, malabar neem (4.40 m) displayed the highest horizontal root spread, while subabul (3.73m) and kadam (3.73*m*) had a similar horizontal spread. In the case of kadam and malabar neem, root spread was less than canopy spread, but it was equal in the case of subabul ([Table 1](#T1)). The number of secondary, tertiary and quaternary roots varied among all tree species. Secondary root number varied from 16.0 (subabul) to 25.0 (kadam); tertiary root from 7.0 (subabul) to 10.6 (malabar neem); and quaternary roots from 0.0 in subabul to in 6.2 in malabar neem. Quaternary root numbers were only observed in malabar neem and kadam. Tree species had a difference in their secondary root length (119.2 to 149.3 cm) and diameter (29.18 to 103.6 cm), with malabar neem being the longest and widest with subabul and kadam following them ([Table 1](#T1)). Tertiary roots also varied in length (84.7-147.5 cm) and diameter (11.9-24.5 cm), following similar trends with malabar neem producing the longest and widest roots, followed by kadam and subabul. The longest and widest quaternary roots were also produced by malabar neem, followed by kadam. Quaternary roots were not present in subabul until three years old. Fine roots (<1.0 mm) in all three tree species had a range of lengths between 20 cm and 32 cm and mostly originated from secondary roots. They were evenly distributed throughout the root zone area. The increment of root diameter was greater than that of root length with age in all three tree species. In the studied species, an 18.1-61.1% increase in length and a 30-63% increase in diameter was observed in three-year-old tap roots over one-year-old tap roots. The diameter of secondary roots exhibited multifold increase i.e. 11.97-48.79 mm, 9.97 to 29.18 mm and 18.40 to 103.59 mm, for kadam, subabul and malabar neem, respectively.([Table 1](#T1)). The corresponding increase in mean root length was 39-252%, while the diameter increased by multiple times (4 to 16). It is axiomatic that the species with deep main roots and more spreading lateral roots take up nutrient and water more efficiently from deeper layers and over a wider area and provide firm anchorage to the tree in soil, thereby making the tree wind firm. [Das and Chaturvedi (2008)](#B18) that *Azadirachta indica* and *Wendlandia exserta* with deep main roots and Moderate lateral root length, can be more efficiently grown in dry conditions. [Sinacore et al. (2017)](#B62) recorded maximum and mean horizontal rooting distance for six species (*Anacardium excelsum, Cedrela odorata, Dalbergia retusa, Pachira quinata, Tabebuia rosea, Terminalia amazonia*) scaled to basal diameter. They found that *T. amazonia* had mean root distances significantly greater than *A. excelsum, C. odorata*, and *P. quinata*. They also compared maximum and mean rooting depth by basal diameter among species and found no significant differences among species for either maximum or mean depth of roots. Compared to subabul and kadam, malabar neem has a larger root spread, which makes it more stable and less vulnerable to wind blow ([Coutts et al., 1999](#B15); [Eamus et al., 2002](#B24); [Barton and Montagu, 2006](#B6)). The rapid growth and maximum stem diameter are the reason for the large and thick primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary roots in malabar neem ([Eamus et al., 2002](#B24); [Barton and Montagu, 2006](#B6)). Malabar neem compact and dense root system may have encouraged an increase in the uptake of nutrients and water. Due to this, it has more diverse types (e.g. Primary/secondary/tertiary, etc.) of roots and a greater potential for producing aboveground biomass. We recorded a concentrated and uniform distribution of fine roots in the root zone, regardless of the tree species. [Yanai et al., 2006](#B68) and [Macinnis-Ng et al., 2010](#B49) also reported that tree fine roots spread uniformly in the root zone. We observed variable root spread corresponding to canopy or crown spread in the three tree species. This indicates that these species are still growing and accumulating aboveground biomass ([Das and Chaturvedi, 2008](#B18)).

### Root angles

The three species had varying angles for secondary, tertiary, and quaternary roots. The three-year study revealed a significant (*P>0.05*) and relatively higher secondary root angle in all three species compared to tertiary and quaternary roots ([Table 1](#T1)). This could have resulted in a greater spread of secondary roots than tertiary and quaternary roots. When compared, the angle of secondary roots in the topsoil was more inclined than those in the deep subsoil. Secondary and tertiary roots showed angles that ranged from 20° to 90°, and there were significant differences among the three species. Quaternary roots also showed wide variation in angles that ranged from 20° to 60°, and were observed only in two tree species, namely malabar neem and kadam. Subabul tree species lacked any quaternary roots ([Table 1](#T1)). Root angles vary due to the tendency of primary, secondary

enriched_points.expanded_content: Root systems anchor plants, enhance water and nutrient uptake, and contribute significantly to soil carbon storage through rhizodeposition and organic matter formation.
Soil organic carbon increases through root biomass decay, promoting structure, nutrient retention, and microbial activity essential for long-term carbon sequestration.
Roots interact with soil microbes, facilitating nutrient cycling and organic matter stabilization, which enhances soil health and boosts carbon storage potential.
Root contributions improve soil fertility by adding organic matter, enhancing microbial diversity, and fostering conditions for nutrient availability and retention.
Deep-rooted plants promote stable carbon pools in subsoil, reducing atmospheric CO2 levels and enhancing resilience against climate-induced stresses.
